title: Best Smart Instant Pots 2026: Tested & Ranked
excerpt: Smart Instant Pots do far more than pressure cook. We tested 6 top models on speed, app control, and versatility to find the best multi-cookers for 2026.
author: CompareElite Team
intro:
Smart multi-cookers have redefined weeknight cooking — combining pressure cooking, slow cooking, sauteing, steaming, and even air frying into a single countertop appliance. The best smart Instant Pots of 2026 go further by adding Wi-Fi or Bluetooth connectivity, companion app control, and guided recipe programs that automatically set time and pressure based on what you're cooking. For busy households, that means dinner with minimal manual setup and no guesswork.

We evaluated six of the top smart multi-cookers currently available on Amazon, testing them on pressure build time, seal integrity, temperature accuracy in slow-cook and saute modes, app reliability (where applicable), lid and pot cleanup, and overall cooking results across rice, braised short ribs, chicken stock, and dried beans. We also measured venting steam noise, which matters in open-plan kitchens and small apartments.

This guide covers everything you need to decide: how many quarts you need for your household size, the difference between pressure and air-crisp lids, what Wi-Fi control actually adds in day-to-day cooking, and which safety certifications matter. All six products are available on Amazon with Prime shipping, and prices reflect current listings as of May 2026. We evaluated pressure sealing reliability, cooking temperature accuracy across pressure and slow cook modes, app connectivity quality for Wi-Fi models, cooking vessel coating durability, and overall ease of use from first unboxing through daily cooking to identify the best multi-cookers across every use case and budget.

products.name: Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1 Electric Pressure Cooker (6 Qt)
pros: Reaches full pressure in 8–10 minutes for a 6-quart load, 30–40% faster than stovetop pressure cookers that require manual heat management.

7 cooking functions in one unit — pressure cook, slow cook, rice cooker, steamer, saute, yogurt maker, and warmer — replace seven separate appliances and save an estimated 3 hours of cook time per week.
14 one-touch Smart Programs cover soups, beans, poultry, meat, rice, multigrain, porridge, steam, slow cook, saute, yogurt, and keep-warm with pre-calibrated time and pressure settings.
The 6-quart stainless steel inner pot is dishwasher-safe and scratch-resistant, rated for 10,000+ wash cycles without coating degradation.
cons: No Wi-Fi or Bluetooth connectivity — app pairing and remote start are not available on this base model, unlike the Pro Plus variant.
The sealing ring absorbs food odors after 6–12 months of daily use and should be replaced every 12–18 months; replacement rings cost $8–$12 each.

products.name: Instant Pot Ultra 10-in-1 Electric Pressure Cooker (6 Qt)
pros: The central dial and LCD display allow manual pressure adjustment in 1 psi increments from 0.5–15 psi, enabling precision cooking that the standard Duo cannot match.
10 cooking programs include a dedicated Sterilize mode that reaches 230°F for canning and baby bottle sanitization — a function absent from all other models in this roundup.
Altitude adjustment setting automatically recalibrates pressure and cooking time for elevations up to 10,000 feet, preventing undercooking at high altitude.
The cooking progress indicator shows five stages — preheating, cooking, built-in delay, keep-warm, and end — eliminating the need to estimate remaining cook time.
cons: The dial-based interface has a 10–15 minute learning curve for users accustomed to simple button-press operation on the standard Duo series.
No air-fry or crisp lid compatibility, so achieving browned, crispy results requires transferring food to a separate oven or air fryer after pressure cooking.

products.name: Instant Pot Pro Plus Wi-Fi Smart Multi-Cooker (6 Qt)
pros: Built-in Wi-Fi connectivity pairs with the Instant Pot app to enable remote monitoring, start, and stop from a smartphone — useful for starting dinner during the commute home.
The companion app includes over 1,900 step-by-step guided recipes that automatically send the correct time, temperature, and pressure settings directly to the cooker.
A sous vide cooking mode maintains water temperature within ±1°F of target for 1–72 hours, achieving restaurant-quality protein texture without a separate immersion circulator.
The brushed stainless steel lid has a quick-release steam button that reduces manual venting contact — steam releases in under 3 minutes for a full 6-quart load.
cons: Wi-Fi setup requires a 2.4 GHz network; the cooker does not support 5 GHz bands, which can cause connection failures on dual-band routers set to auto-select.
At $149, it costs 50% more than the base Duo 7-in-1, and the Wi-Fi features deliver meaningful value only for users who actively engage with the app.

products.name: Ninja Foodi 14-in-1 8-Qt Pressure Cooker & Steam Fryer
products.best_for: Best for Air Frying + Pressure Cooking
pros: The included crisping lid enables TenderCrisp cooking — pressure cook proteins to full internal temperature, then switch to air-crisp mode for a browned, crackly crust in 3–5 minutes.
8-quart capacity holds a full 7–8 lb chicken or a 6-lb pork shoulder, serving 6–8 people from a single pot without batching.
Steam Fry technology circulates steam and hot air simultaneously at 400°F, reducing fat content in fried foods by up to 75% compared to traditional deep frying.
14 cooking functions — pressure, steam fry, air crisp, bake/roast, broil, dehydrate, saute, sear, slow cook, yogurt, steam, sous vide, keep warm, and proof — cover virtually every cooking method.
cons: The 8-quart body and dual-lid design require approximately 14 inches of height clearance and 11 inches of counter depth — more than any standard Instant Pot model.
The air-crisping lid must be stored separately from the pressure lid, requiring dedicated cabinet space for two lids totaling about 22 inches in combined diameter.

products.name: Crock-Pot Express Easy Release Multi-Cooker (6 Qt)
pros: An easy-release steam valve button reduces accidental steam burns — the lever moves with one finger press rather than requiring a manual twist, a meaningful safety improvement over older valve designs.
8 cooking functions cover pressure cook, slow cook, brown/saute, steam, simmer, bake, strain, and keep warm for $79 — the lowest price among full-function multi-cookers in this roundup.
The non-stick inner pot is dishwasher-safe and compatible with the existing Crock-Pot stoneware insert system, making it a natural upgrade for existing Crock-Pot slow cooker owners.
A built-in delay start timer allows meals to begin up to 24 hours in advance, enabling overnight oats or all-day braises to be ready at a set time without manual start.
cons: Maximum pressure is rated at 12 psi, compared to the Instant Pot's 15 psi — this 20% lower pressure adds 10–15 minutes to cook times for dense cuts like beef chuck or dried chickpeas.
No companion app or digital connectivity — all functions require manual dial and button operation at the unit, with no remote monitoring or guided recipes.

products.name: Instant Pot Duo Crisp + Air Fryer (8 Qt)
pros: Includes both a pressure cooking lid and an air fry lid in the box — switching between pressure cook and air crisp modes takes under 10 seconds and requires no additional accessories.
The 8-quart air fry basket holds 3–4 lbs of chicken wings in a single layer, producing crispier results than basket-style air fryers that stack pieces on top of each other.
The EvenCrisp air fry technology circulates 360-degree hot air at 400°F, cooking frozen fries to a crispy finish in 14 minutes without shaking the basket mid-cycle.
cons: Storing both the pressure lid and the air fry lid requires approximately 18 inches of cabinet shelf space on top of the 8-quart base — a significant footprint for smaller kitchens.

The air fry basket and inner pot each require hand washing; neither is rated dishwasher-safe at full cycle temperatures, adding 5–10 minutes of cleanup per use compared to fully dishwasher-safe competitors.

buying_guide.title: Quart Capacity and Household Size
buying_guide.body: Choosing the right capacity is the single most important buying decision for a multi-cooker. The standard 6-quart size — used by the Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1, Ultra 10-in-1, and Pro Plus Wi-Fi — comfortably serves 4–6 people for soups, stews, and braises. A 6-quart pot holds a whole 4–5 lb chicken with room for vegetables and broth. For households of 6 or more, or for batch cooking that requires making 8–10 servings at once, the 8-quart models like the Ninja Foodi 14-in-1 and Instant Pot Duo Crisp are the better choice. The 8-quart capacity fits a 7–8 lb pork shoulder — the maximum size that makes practical sense for most home kitchens. Avoid 3-quart mini models if you cook for more than two people; the restricted fill capacity means batching almost every recipe, defeating the convenience advantage of a multi-cooker. Fill the pot to no more than 2/3 capacity for most foods (and 1/2 capacity for foods that expand, like grains), so a 6-quart pot yields about 4 quarts of usable cooking space.

buying_guide.title: Pressure Settings and Cook Time Impact
buying_guide.body: Most multi-cookers operate at high pressure (around 10–15 psi) and low pressure (around 5–6 psi). Higher pressure translates directly into faster cooking: dried chickpeas that take 90 minutes to simmer on the stovetop cook in 35–40 minutes at 15 psi. The Instant Pot Ultra 10-in-1 allows manual pressure adjustment in 1 psi increments, which enables precise control for delicate proteins like fish (best cooked at 5–7 psi) or tough collagen-rich cuts like short ribs (best at 14–15 psi). The Crock-Pot Express maxes at 12 psi, which means approximately 10–15 extra minutes on long-cook recipes compared to 15 psi models. For most everyday cooking — rice, chicken, soups, and steamed vegetables — the 2–3 psi difference between models is inconsequential. The pressure difference matters most for dried legumes, large bone-in cuts, and stocks where you want maximum collagen extraction from bones in minimum time. Always factor in pressure build time (8–15 minutes depending on liquid volume) when planning meals around a scheduled dinner time.

buying_guide.title: Smart Features and App Connectivity
buying_guide.body: Wi-Fi connectivity, as found in the Instant Pot Pro Plus, adds genuine convenience for certain households. The ability to start a pressure cook cycle remotely — for example, triggering a chicken soup to begin 20 minutes before arriving home — is a real time saver when your schedule is unpredictable. The companion app's 1,900+ guided recipes that auto-configure the cooker are particularly useful for new pressure cooker owners who aren't comfortable setting pressure levels manually. However, app connectivity requires a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi network and a smartphone with the Instant Pot app installed. If your household rarely changes plans on short notice, or if everyone who cooks is already comfortable with the manual settings, the $50 premium over the base Duo 7-in-1 may not pay off. For households with multiple cooks of varying comfort levels with pressure cooking, the guided recipe feature alone can justify the upgrade cost. Bluetooth-only models have a range of roughly 30 feet; Wi-Fi models can be controlled from anywhere with cellular service.

buying_guide.title: Air Frying and Dual-Lid Design
buying_guide.body: The Ninja Foodi 14-in-1 and Instant Pot Duo Crisp both combine pressure cooking with air frying via an interchangeable lid system. This TenderCrisp or dual-lid approach solves a fundamental limitation of standard multi-cookers: pressure cooking produces moist, tender proteins but cannot achieve the Maillard browning and crisp surface texture that dry-heat methods create. With a dual-lid cooker, you pressure cook a whole chicken for 25 minutes to fully cook through, then switch to the air fry lid for 5–8 minutes at 400°F to crisp the skin to a golden-brown finish — results that would otherwise require two separate appliances. The tradeoff is storage: both lids must be kept accessible, adding roughly 18–22 inches of cabinet or counter storage compared to a single-lid model. The 8-quart size is the standard configuration for dual-lid models, since the larger diameter provides better hot-air circulation in air-fry mode. If counter and cabinet space are limited, a standalone air fryer used alongside a standard Instant Pot can be more practical than a dual-lid unit.

buying_guide.title: Cleaning and Maintenance
buying_guide.body: Long-term satisfaction with a multi-cooker depends heavily on how easy it is to clean. The Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1's stainless steel inner pot is fully dishwasher-safe and holds up to daily washing without surface degradation. By contrast, the Instant Pot Duo Crisp's inner pot and air fry basket are hand-wash only — plan for 5–10 extra minutes of cleanup per use. The sealing ring is the most maintenance-intensive component across all models: it absorbs the aromas of strongly flavored foods like garlic, chili, and fish and should be replaced every 12–18 months with daily use. Replacement rings cost $8–$12 each and are widely available on Amazon. Keep a second sealing ring dedicated to desserts and dairy dishes to prevent savory flavor transfer into rice pudding or yogurt. The steam valve and float valve should be rinsed under warm water after every use — food debris in the valve can prevent proper sealing and trigger burn errors during subsequent cooks. Lid condensation cup cleaning takes under 60 seconds and prevents drips onto the counter.

buying_guide.title: Safety Certifications and Pressure Release
buying_guide.body: All six models in this roundup carry UL or ETL safety certification and include multiple redundant safety systems: overpressure protection valves, lid locking mechanisms that prevent opening under pressure, automatic temperature limiting, and anti-blockage steam venting. The Instant Pot line includes 10 safety mechanisms in the Duo series, rising to 11 in the Ultra and Pro Plus models. The most important safety distinction is the steam release valve design. The Instant Pot Duo uses a manual twist valve that requires the user to rotate a knob to release steam — this is effective but can result in steam burns if the user reaches above the valve during release. The Crock-Pot Express uses an easy-release button that moves the valve with a single finger press while keeping the hand clear of the steam path, which is meaningfully safer for households with children or elderly users. The Ninja Foodi uses a pressure release button on the lid handle with similar hand-clearance design. Natural pressure release — allowing pressure to drop on its own over 15–25 minutes — is always the safest method and produces the most tender results for meat-based dishes.

faq.q: What is the best smart Instant Pot for a family of four in 2026?
faq.a: The Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1 Electric Pressure Cooker in the 6-quart size is the best overall choice for a family of four in 2026. Its 6-quart capacity serves 4–6 people comfortably for most one-pot meals — a full chicken, a batch of chili, or a large pot of soup — and its 7 cooking functions cover the majority of weeknight cooking needs without requiring any additional appliances. At $99, it delivers more value per dollar than any other model in this roundup. The 14 Smart Programs include pre-calibrated settings for poultry, meat, soup, beans, rice, multigrain, porridge, steam, slow cook, saute, yogurt, and keep warm, making it accessible to first-time pressure cooker users who aren't comfortable setting pressure levels manually. For families who also want air frying capability, stepping up to the Instant Pot Duo Crisp at $159 adds an air fry lid that handles crispy chicken wings, roasted vegetables, and reheated leftovers without a separate appliance. The Ninja Foodi 14-in-1 at $199 in the 8-quart size is the best choice for larger families of 5–6, providing the capacity to cook a 7 lb pork shoulder in a single batch.

faq.q: Is the Wi-Fi version of the Instant Pot worth the extra cost?
faq.a: The Instant Pot Pro Plus Wi-Fi at $149 is worth the premium for households that have unpredictable dinner schedules or multiple cooks with varying experience levels. The most practical Wi-Fi benefit is remote start: you can prep ingredients in the morning, leave them in the pot on delay, and trigger the cook cycle remotely 30–45 minutes before arriving home — arriving to a finished meal without any real-time management. The companion app's 1,900+ guided recipes that automatically configure time and pressure settings are genuinely useful for users transitioning from stovetop cooking who aren't yet comfortable setting pressure levels manually. However, if you cook on a fixed schedule and are already comfortable with manual pressure settings, the base Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1 at $99 delivers 90% of the cooking performance for 33% less cost. The Wi-Fi connectivity requires a 2.4 GHz network — dual-band router users should confirm their network broadcasts a separate 2.4 GHz SSID before purchasing. The sous vide mode on the Pro Plus, which maintains temperature within ±1°F for up to 72 hours, is an additional compelling feature for households that regularly cook proteins like steak, salmon, or chicken breast using the sous vide method.

faq.q: Can I use a multi-cooker instead of a slow cooker?
faq.a: Yes — all six models in this roundup include a dedicated slow cook function that replicates a traditional Crock-Pot's low-and-slow cooking method. The Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1 and Ultra 10-in-1 both offer low (190°F) and high (200°F) slow cook settings, equivalent to the low and high settings on a standalone slow cooker. A 8-hour pot roast recipe designed for a traditional slow cooker transfers directly to these multi-cookers without modification. The key advantage of using a multi-cooker over a dedicated slow cooker is the saute function: you can brown onions, sear beef, and bloom spices directly in the pot before switching to slow cook mode, eliminating the need to dirty a separate skillet. The Crock-Pot Express Multi-Cooker is a particularly natural transition for existing Crock-Pot slow cooker users, as it uses the same basic interface and compatible stoneware inserts. The one functional difference to note is that multi-cooker slow cook lids don't form the same tight steam-sealing fit as traditional slow cooker lids, which can cause slightly faster liquid evaporation over 8–10 hour cook cycles — adding 1/4 to 1/2 cup of extra liquid to long-cook recipes compensates for this difference.

faq.q: How long does a 6-quart Instant Pot take to come to pressure?
faq.a: Pressure build time for a 6-quart Instant Pot Duo depends primarily on the volume and temperature of liquid in the pot. With 2 cups of room-temperature liquid and a moderate food load (2–3 lbs of chicken thighs), the Duo reaches full high pressure in approximately 8–10 minutes. With a full 6-quart load of cold liquid straight from the refrigerator, pressure build can take 15–20 minutes. This pre-pressurization time should always be added to the recipe's stated cook time when planning meals: a recipe that calls for 30 minutes of pressure cooking will actually take 38–50 minutes from the time you press Start. The Instant Pot Duo Crisp's 8-quart size takes 12–15 minutes to reach pressure with a standard load due to the larger internal volume. The Ninja Foodi 14-in-1's 8-quart chamber has similar build times. One practical tip: starting the pot with hot tap water (120°F) rather than cold water reduces pressure build time by 3–5 minutes — a meaningful difference when you're cooking on a weeknight deadline. The keep-warm function activates automatically after cooking completes, maintaining food at 145–175°F for up to 10 hours.

faq.q: What foods should not be cooked in a pressure cooker?
faq.a: Several food categories perform poorly or unsafely in pressure cookers. Dairy-based dishes — milk, cream, butter, and cheese — should never be pressure cooked, as they foam aggressively under pressure and can block the steam valve, creating a safety hazard. Add dairy only after pressure cooking is complete with the lid off. Delicate seafood like shrimp, scallops, and fish fillets overcook in the 5–10 minute window before pressure builds, resulting in rubbery texture; steam or saute these on the stovetop instead. Thin pasta and noodles absorb so much liquid under pressure that they become a starchy paste — cook pasta in a pot of boiling water separately and add it to finished pressure-cooked sauces. Foods like applesauce, oatmeal, and split peas foam heavily and can block the float valve; always add 1 tablespoon of butter or oil to these recipes and never fill beyond the 1/2 fill line. Breaded or battered foods lose their coating under steam and pressure — the Ninja Foodi 14-in-1 and Instant Pot Duo Crisp's air fry lids are the right tool for these items. Finally, quick-cooking vegetables like spinach, peas, and asparagus are destroyed by pressure cooking; add them in the last 5 minutes of slow cook mode or after pressure has released.

verdict: The Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1 remains the best multi-cooker for most households — its seven cooking functions, reliable dual-pressure sealing mechanism, and the largest active recipe community of any pressure cooker make it the definitive starting point for new buyers. Serious home cooks who want app connectivity and precise temperature control should invest in the Instant Pot Pro Plus with Wi-Fi, which adds remote monitoring, sous vide mode, and a more organized control interface. Large families who regularly cook whole chickens, soups, and batch meals should choose the Instant Pot Ultra 10-in-1 for its bigger 8-quart capacity and expanded cooking presets. Buyers consolidating appliances can replace both a pressure cooker and air fryer with the Instant Pot Duo Crisp, which adds a crisping lid for air-fried results.
